title: Social Media Usage and Depression in Adolescents: A Longitudinal Study
abstract: This 3-year longitudinal study tracked 2,847 adolescents aged 13-17 to examine the relationship between social media usage and depression symptoms. Participants with over 3 hours daily social media use showed 23% higher depression scores compared to those with under 1 hour usage. Instagram and TikTok showed stronger correlations with depressive symptoms than Facebook or Twitter. However, the study found that passive consumption (scrolling) was more harmful than active engagement (posting, commenting). Adolescents who used social media for educational content or hobby groups showed no increased depression risk. The research suggests that usage patterns matter more than total time spent on platforms.
